english: 'Nach' (after) + 'vollziehbar' (executable/comprehensible). You can trace the logic step by step. The opposite of impenetrable Amtsdeutsch. Every good Bescheid should be nachvollziehbar.
Adjective. 'Recht' + '-lich.'\n'Rechtliche Schritte einleiten' (to take legal action).
'Recht' + '-lich.' The general adjective for anything law-related. 'Rechtlich gesehen...' (From a legal perspective...). The neutral word that frames everything in legal terms.
Past participle of 'genehmigen.' Approved.\n'Der Antrag ist genehmigt.' (Zustandspassiv in action.)
Past participle of 'genehmigen.' Zustandspassiv in action: 'Der Antrag ist genehmigt.' The word you want to see on any bureaucratic response. The green light of German officialdom.
+ genitive. 'Bezueglich Ihres Schreibens vom 10. Mai...'\nAbbreviated: 'bzgl.'
From 'Bezug' (reference) + '-lich.' The standard opening of bureaucratic correspondence. You will find 'bezüglich' in the first line of nearly every official German letter.
+ genitive. 'Hinsichtlich der neuen Verordnung...'\nSlightly more formal than bezüglich.
From 'Hinsicht' (regard, respect) + '-lich.' Used in legal and academic texts. The elevated 'regarding' for when bezüglich is not formal enough.
+ genitive. 'Aufgrund Ihrer Beschwerde...'\nAlso written 'auf Grund' (two words).
'Auf' (on) + 'Grund' (ground/reason). Standing on the ground of your complaint. The causal genitive preposition of legal German.
+ genitive. 'Infolge des Beschlusses...'\nMore consequential than aufgrund.
'In' + 'Folge' (consequence). The direct chain: this happened BECAUSE of that. More emphatic than aufgrund. The preposition that traces cause to effect.
+ genitive. 'Vorbehaltlich der Genehmigung...'\nNothing is final until the condition is met.
From 'Vorbehalt' (reservation) + '-lich.' The conditional preposition: IF this is approved. Adds a legal safety clause. Nothing is guaranteed with vorbehaltlich.
+ dative (postposed): 'dem Gesetz zufolge.'\n+ genitive (preposed): 'zufolge des Gesetzes.'
'Zu' (to) + 'Folge' (following). The attribution preposition: who says so? Postposed with dative is more common. Preposed with genitive is more formal.
+ dative or genitive. 'Laut Paragraph 5...'\nShort and efficient.
Originally from 'Laut' (sound). Now: 'according to what it says.' Short, efficient, bureaucratic. The most compact way to cite a source in German.
Prepositional phrase. + accusative.\n'Unter Bezugnahme auf Ihr Schreiben vom...'
The most bureaucratic prepositional phrase in German. It connects your response to their letter. A cross-reference machine that tells the reader: I am responding to THIS document.
Fixed legal phrase. + genitive.\n'Im Sinne des Paragraph 5 des Aufenthaltsgesetzes...'
Not your personal interpretation. The law's interpretation. This phrase removes all ambiguity. When you read 'im Sinne des Gesetzes,' the definition that follows is legally binding.
Citation formula. Paragraph number = legal authority.\n'Gemaess Paragraph 23 haben Sie das Recht auf...'
The citation formula of German law. Paragraph number equals legal authority. No opinion, only law. Every German court ruling is built on chains of 'gemaess Paragraph' references.
Fixed legal phrase. 'Hiermit erhebe ich Einspruch\ngegen den Bescheid vom...'
'Erheben' means to raise or elevate. You lift your objection up for the court to see. The formal legal act of objecting. Always schriftlich (in writing).
Complete opening sentence of a formal objection letter.\n'Hiermit' (hereby) + 'einlegen' (to file/lodge).
The phrase that starts the legal process of challenging a Bescheid. Memorize this sentence, and you can write the opening of any German legal objection letter.
Feminine noun. From 'anlegen' (to attach/create).\n'Siehe Anlage 1' (See attachment 1).
Also means: a facility, a system, or an investment. In letters: the documents attached. 'Anlage' is printed at the bottom of every official letter to list what is enclosed.
Feminine noun. Amt + Sprache (language).\nAlso: the language STYLE of the Amt.
Article 23 of the Verwaltungsverfahrensgesetz: 'Die Amtssprache ist Deutsch.' All government communication must be in German. No exceptions. Translations are your responsibility.
Feminine noun. Beitrag (contribution) + Ordnung.\n'Gemaess der Beitragsordnung...'
Rules about who pays how much. Every club, insurance, and institution has a Beitragsordnung. German love of Ordnung applied to money.
Feminine noun. Nach (after) + Frist (deadline).\n'Eine Nachfrist von 14 Tagen wird gewährt.'
You missed the first Frist. Here is your second chance. Do not miss this one. A Nachfrist is a gift from the bureaucracy. They rarely grant a third.
Feminine noun. From 'vergueten' (to compensate).\nMore formal than Gehalt (salary).
From 'vergueten' (to compensate). Used in contracts and official documents. More formal than Gehalt. Public sector employees receive Verguetung according to fixed pay scales.
Logical connector. 'Demnach ist die Frist einzuhalten.'\nShort, authoritative, legal register.
'Dem' (that) + 'nach' (after/according to). Given the above, this follows. Short and conclusive. The lawyer's 'therefore.'
Formal connector. 'Ferner ist zu beachten, dass...'\nMore formal than ausserdem.
'Fern' (far) + comparative. The formal 'also.' Legal and academic register. When 'ausserdem' is too casual, 'ferner' steps in.
Also used as adjective. + dative.\n'Entsprechend der Vorschrift...'
From 'entsprechen' (to correspond). The preposition of conformity: matching what is prescribed. As adjective: 'die entsprechenden Unterlagen' (the corresponding documents).
Masculine noun. From 'vertragen' (to bear/agree).\nPlural: Verträge. 'Einen Vertrag unterschreiben.'
Both sides bear (tragen) the agreement together. 'Mietvertrag' (rental), 'Arbeitsvertrag' (employment), 'Kaufvertrag' (purchase). Every major life event in Germany involves a Vertrag.
Feminine noun. From 'kündigen' (to give notice).\n'Die Kuendigung des Vertrags' (contract termination).
German notice periods are among the strictest in Europe. Written, signed, fristgerecht. A verbal Kuendigung has no legal force. Always keep a copy.
Feminine noun. Voll (full) + Macht (power).\n'Eine Vollmacht erteilen' (to grant power of attorney).
Full power given to someone to act on your behalf. Legal authority transferred. Without a Vollmacht, no one can access your files at the Behörde. Not even your spouse.
Masculine noun. Plural: Maengel.\n'Einen Mangel reklamieren' (to complain about a defect).
From Latin 'mancus' (deficient). In consumer law: if something has a Mangel, you have rights. Gewährleistung (warranty) covers Maengel for two years.
Masculine noun. Plural: Schaeden.\n'Einen Schaden melden' (to report damage).
Insurance vocabulary: Schaden is what happened, Schadensersatz is what you get. 'Sachschaden' (property damage) vs. 'Personenschaden' (injury). Germany's insurance culture revolves around Schaeden.
Feminine noun. From Latin 'reclamare' (to cry out against).\n'Eine Reklamation einreichen.'
The consumer's weapon. You have two years for Reklamation in Germany. Something is wrong? File a Reklamation. The law is on your side.
Feminine noun. Ab (off) + Mahnung (warning).\nIn employment: precedes Kuendigung.
The yellow card before the red. In employment law, an Abmahnung precedes Kuendigung. In consumer law: a cease-and-desist letter. Always take an Abmahnung seriously.
Feminine noun. Dienst (service) + Leistung (performance).\n'Dienstleistungsvertrag' (service contract).
Germany's economy is 70% Dienstleistungen. The compound says it: serving + performing. 'Dienstleistungsgesellschaft' (service economy) describes modern Germany.
Masculine noun. From 'betreffen' (to concern).\n'Betreff: Ihr Antrag vom 10. Mai.'
Every German official letter begins with Betreff. It tells the reader what the letter is about before they read a single sentence. Precision before content.
Masculine noun. Leser (reader) + Brief (letter).\nHildi's weapon of choice.
Hildi's weapon of choice: disagreeing publicly through the newspaper. German Leserbriefe have a long tradition. Many local papers have a full page dedicated to them.
Feminine noun. Auf (up) + Gabe (giving).\nAlso means 'giving up' (surrender).
Dual meaning: what you must do (task) or what you stop doing (surrender). 'Aufgabe' in bureaucracy always means duty. In chess, 'Aufgabe' means resignation. Context decides.
Feminine noun. An + Forderung (demand).\n'Die Anforderungen erfuellen' (to meet the requirements).
What the system demands of you before it acts. Anforderungen are non-negotiable. Missing one Anforderung can delay your entire Antrag by weeks.
Not separable. 'Einen Ausweis beantragen.'\nThe verb form of Antrag.
You beantragen, and the Behörde bewilligt (or ablehnt). The beginning of every bureaucratic journey. Without beantragen, nothing happens in Germany.
Not separable. 'Ihr Antrag wird bearbeitet.'\nThe Sachbearbeiter bearbeitet your case.
The process between submission and decision. 'Be-' + 'arbeiten' (to work). The Sachbearbeiter bearbeitet. When you hear 'Ihr Antrag wird bearbeitet,' prepare to wait.
Separable. Ein (in) + reichen (to hand/reach).\n'Den Antrag einreichen.'
You reach the document INTO the system. 'Fristgerecht eingereicht' means submitted on time. Missing the deadline means your submission does not count.
Not separable. From 'genehm' (agreeable).\n'Den Antrag genehmigen.'
The Behörde finds your request genehm (agreeable) and says yes. The most welcome word in bureaucracy. 'Genehmigt' stamped on your Antrag is the German equivalent of a green light.
Separable. Aus (out) + stellen (to place).\n'Einen Bescheid ausstellen' (to issue a notice).
The document is placed OUT of the system into your hands. Also used for exhibitions: 'Ein Bild ausstellen' (to exhibit a painting). Two meanings: to create an official document, or to display art.
Not separable. From 'staetigen' (to make firm).\n'Den Empfang bestätigen' (to confirm receipt).
Making something firm, certain, official. Your signature bestätigt your identity. An Eingangsbestätigung (confirmation of receipt) is your proof that the Behörde has your documents.
Separable. An (at) + ordnen (to arrange).\n'Die Behörde ordnet an, dass...'
An official command from an authority. Stronger than 'bitten' (to request). When the Behörde anordnet, it is not a suggestion. Compliance is expected.
Not separable. Be- + Auftrag (commission/task).\n'Einen Rechtsanwalt beauftragen.'
You give someone an official Auftrag. Beauftragen is how professionals are hired in German legal culture. Without Beauftragung, no one acts on your behalf.
Separable. Ein (in) + legen (to lay).\n'Widerspruch einlegen' (to file an objection).
You lay your objection INTO the legal process. 'Widerspruch einlegen,' 'Berufung einlegen,' 'Einspruch einlegen.' The verb for entering the legal arena.
Not separable. Er- + lassen (to let).\nDual meaning: creating rules OR releasing from obligation.
Creating rules or releasing from them. 'Eine Verordnung erlassen' (to issue an ordinance). 'Die Schulden erlassen' (to waive the debts). The same verb, opposite effects.
Not separable. Unter + sagen (to say).\nMore formal than 'verbieten.'
The official word for banning something. More formal than 'verbieten.' When the Behörde untersagt, it is a legal act with consequences.
Not separable. Ver- + Anlass (occasion/cause).\n'Bitte veranlassen Sie die Überweisung.'
You cause something to happen through official channels. Not doing it yourself, but setting it in motion. The bureaucratic 'make it happen.'
Not separable. Ver- + fuegen (to join/dispose).\nDual meaning: to command AND to possess.
Dual meaning: to command AND to possess. 'Über etwas verfügen' (to have at one's disposal). 'Eine Schliessung verfügen' (to decree a closure). Context determines everything.
Not separable. Ver- + weigern (to refuse).\nStronger than ablehnen.
Stronger than ablehnen. Official refusal with legal consequences. When permission is verweigert, you need a Rechtsbehelf (legal remedy) to fight back.
Not separable. Voll (full) + strecken (to stretch).\nThe final step of legal enforcement.
The law stretches out fully and is applied. The final step: after Urteil comes Vollstreckung. 'Zwangsvollstreckung' (forced enforcement) is the last resort.
Separable. Vor (before) + sehen (to see).\n'Das Gesetz sieht vor, dass...'
The law looked ahead and planned for this. 'Vorsehen' in legal language: the law stipulates, provides, foresees. What the law vorsieht, you must follow.
Separable. Zurück (back) + weisen (to direct).\n'Den Einspruch zurückweisen.'
The authority sends your objection back to you. Rejected. Stronger than ablehnen. When something is zurückgewiesen, the process is over at this level.
Separable. Zu (to) + stellen (to place).\nLegal delivery that starts Fristen.
Legal delivery: the moment of Zustellung starts all Fristen. A letter that is zugestellt has legal force. 'Zustellung per Einschreiben' (delivery by registered mail) is the standard.
Separable. Zu (to) + stimmen (to vote/tune).\n+ dative: 'dem Antrag zustimmen.'
You tune your voice TO the proposal. + dative. When the Behörde zustimmt, your Antrag is on the way to Genehmigung.
Not separable. Wider (against) + rufen (to call).\n'Die Genehmigung widerrufen.'
You call AGAINST your own previous statement. A Widerruf cancels what was granted before. 'Widerrufsrecht' (right of withdrawal) gives consumers 14 days to cancel online purchases.
Regular -ieren verb. Consumer rights vocabulary.\n'Ein defektes Produkt reklamieren.'
From Latin 'reclamare.' Consumer rights vocabulary. You have two years for Reklamation in Germany. Always keep the Kassenbon (receipt).
Regular -ieren verb. International law vocabulary.\n'Den Vertrag ratifizieren.'
From Latin 'ratum facere' (to make settled). A formal confirmation that makes an agreement binding. Only used for international treaties and major agreements.
Separable. Statt (place) + geben (to give).\n'Dem Einspruch stattgeben' (to uphold the objection).
The court gives the objection its place: granted. The opposite of zurückweisen. When a Gericht stattgibt, you win.
Separable. Nach (after) + kommen (to come).\n'Der Aufforderung nachkommen.'
You come AFTER the request, fulfilling it. + dative. When the Behörde fordert, you kommen nach. Compliance is not optional.
Regular verb. The verb behind Mahnung.\n'An die Zahlung mahnen.'
Three Mahnungen, then legal action. The escalation system of German payment culture. First friendly, then firm, then final.
Not separable. From 'Kunde' (notice/knowledge).\n'Den Vertrag kündigen.'
'Jemandem kündigen' (to fire someone). 'Selber kündigen' (to quit). German Kuendigungsfristen (notice periods) are among the longest in Europe. Always in writing.
Separable. Bei (by/with) + legen (to lay).\nTwo meanings: attach OR resolve.
Two meanings: attach (to a letter) or resolve (a dispute). 'Die Unterlagen beilegen' (to enclose documents). 'Den Streit beilegen' (to settle the dispute). Same verb, different contexts.
Pflicht (duty) + gemaess (in accordance with).\n'Pflichtgemaess erfuellt.'
You did what duty required. The bureaucratic seal of compliance. 'Pflichtgemaess' is the adjective that confirms: all rules were followed.
Past participle of einreichen.\n'Der eingereichte Antrag' (the submitted application).
Zustandspassiv ready: 'Der Antrag ist eingereicht' (the application has been submitted). The past participle that confirms: the document is in the system.
Neuter noun. Grund (land/property) + Buch (book).\nEvery property is recorded in the Grundbuch.
Every piece of property in Germany is recorded in the Grundbuch. When you buy a house, the Grundbuchamt records the new owner. No Grundbucheintrag, no ownership.
Masculine noun. From 'erlassen.'\nDual meaning: issuing rules OR releasing from obligation.
A decree OR a waiver. 'Ein Erlass des Ministers' (a ministerial decree). 'Erlass der Schulden' (debt waiver). Creating rules or releasing from them. Same noun, opposite effects.
Strong verb: entscheiden/entschied/entschieden.\n'Die Behörde entscheidet über den Antrag.'
Literally: to cut apart the options. 'Ent-' (away) + 'scheiden' (to separate). Strong verb. The Behörde entscheidet, and the Bescheid tells you the Entscheidung.
Not separable. From 'Teil' (part).\n'Eine Genehmigung erteilen' (to grant a permit).
The formal way to say 'give officially.' 'Auskunft erteilen' (to provide information). 'Genehmigung erteilen' (to grant a permit). Always formal, always official.
Past participle of 'erledigen' (to take care of).\n'Die Sache ist erledigt' (The matter is done).
The most satisfying word in German bureaucracy. Erledigt means finished, handled, closed. Stamp 'erledigt' on a file, and it is over. The bureaucratic equivalent of 'mission accomplished.'
Past participle of 'bewilligen' (to grant).\n'Der Antrag ist bewilligt.'
Zustandspassiv: the approval is a completed state. You submitted, they approved, it is bewilligt. Celebrate. 'Bewilligt' stamped on your Antrag is the best outcome.
Present participle of 'binden' (to bind).\n'Ein bindender Vertrag' (a binding contract).
Something bindend cannot be ignored without legal consequences. 'Rechtsverbindlich' is the full legal version. A bindender Vertrag holds all parties to their word.
Essen (to eat) + '-bar' (able).\nPattern: verb stem + -bar = '-able.'
The -bar suffix works like English '-able': essbar (edible), lesbar (readable), machbar (doable). One suffix, endless adjectives. Pattern recognition for German word building.
Loesen (to solve) + '-bar.'\n'Das Problem ist loesbar.'
Every bureaucratic problem in Germany is loesbar if you know the right Formular. 'Loesbar' combines loesen (to solve) with -bar (able). The optimistic adjective of the Amt.
Machen (to make/do) + '-bar.'\n'Ist das machbar?'
The pragmatic German question: not 'should we' but 'can we.' Machbar is the green light. 'Machbarkeitsstudie' (feasibility study) checks if something is machbar before anyone starts.
Un- + vorstellbar (imaginable).\n'Die Bürokratie war unvorstellbar.'
The word for when German bureaucracy exceeds even German expectations. Un- + vorstellbar. You cannot even imagine it. And yet it happens. Every day, at every Amt.
In + soweit (so far). Legal precision.\n'Insoweit ist die Lage klar.'
Drawing boundaries around the scope of a statement. To exactly this extent and no further. The lawyer's precision tool.
Mit (with) + hin (there).\n'Mithin ist der Bescheid gueltig.'
Short, conclusive, legal register. The lawyer's 'therefore.' Mithin announces a logical conclusion that follows inevitably from what came before.
Seite (side) + '-ns' (genitive). + genitive.\n'Seitens der Behörde wurde mitgeteilt...'
The attribution preposition: which side did this come from? + genitive. 'Seitens der Regierung' (on the part of the government). Formal, distanced, institutional.
Un- + beschadet (damaged). + genitive.\n'Unbeschadet der Regelung...'
This does not damage or affect the other rule. Both can coexist. Legal language for: the new rule does not cancel the old one. They live side by side.
Nichts (nothing) + desto (the) + weniger (less).\nThe formal twin of nichtsdestotrotz.
The nothing-the-less construction at its most elegant. The formal twin of 'nichtsdestotrotz.' 26 letters of concessive beauty. Despite everything, we continue.
